steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure on each side of the blank
steps.content: Before the blank: a noun completing the object of a preposition within an independent clause. After the blank: an independent clause with its own subject and verb.

steps.title: Apply the rule for joining independent clauses
steps.content:
Two independent clauses surround the blank: "The Limon technique... is known for its emphasis on breath control and its interplay of weight and weightlessness" (IC) and "dancers may explore... the moment of mid-air suspension..." (IC). The rule: two ICs need a semicolon, period, or comma+conjunction — a comma alone is a comma splice.
  • Choice A — "weightlessness;": semicolon between two ICs — grammatically correct. ✓
  • Choice B — "weightlessness which": "which" needs a comma before it to introduce a non-restrictive clause; without the comma, this creates a run-on. ✗
  • Choice C — "weightlessness,": comma alone between two ICs = comma splice. ✗
  • Choice D — "weightlessness": no punctuation at all between two ICs = fused sentence (run-on). ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
